sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Low-risk mental health presentations involve distress or functional impairment without immediate risk of suicide, violence, psychosis, or inability to care for self. Examples include adjustment reactions, situational stress, and mild anxiety or depressive symptoms.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Anxiety, irritability, low mood, or sleep disturbance
- Difficulty concentrating or making decisions
- Mild somatic complaints such as headache or fatigue
- Usually oriented, cooperative, and able to engage in discussion

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Exclude immediate risk factors such as suicidality, self-harm intent, aggression, hallucinations, or serious organic illness
- Use a respectful, non-judgemental approach and assess support systems
- Stay within scope and avoid providing care beyond training or authority
- Document findings and advice clearly

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Provide reassurance, active listening, and validation
- Reduce sensory stimuli and offer a calm environment where possible
- Encourage practical supports and appropriate follow-up pathways such as EAP or local mental health support
- Perform Vital Sign Survey including BGL where relevant
- Escalate to the on-call doctor if risk is uncertain or the presentation is deteriorating

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content: - Sedating medication should not be routine for low-risk presentations and should only be considered within scope and after senior discussion when truly necessary

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content: - Debrief and referral pathways should respect confidentiality and patient autonomy where possible
